root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

manifolds in context: dynamics + mechanics

Table of contents

  1. Vector fields
  2. Dynamical systems, flows, and integral curves
  3. A first glimpse at variational principles: lagrangian mechanics
  4. The cotangent bundle
  5. A first glimpse at symplectic topology: hamiltonian mechanics

Vector fields

Dynamical systems, flows, and integral curves

Manifolds often manifest themselves in physical theories as configuration spaces, encoding the locations and positions (i.e., the configurations) of the various objects under consideration. For example, once an origin and global coordinate system is fixed, the configuration space of a system consisting of a single point mass in $3$-space is $\bbr^3$. More generally, a system consisting of $k$ point masses in $3$-space has configuration space $\bbr^{3k}$, where each particle requires $3$-coordiantes to specify its location. Typographically, configuration spaces are often represented as $Q$, and local coordinates are often written as

\begin{equation}\notag q^1,\ldots,q^n. \end{equation}

Local coordinates on a configuration space $Q$ are often called generalized position coordinates.

Given a fixed configuration $q_0$ in a configuration space $Q$, a smooth path

\begin{equation}\label{pos-coord-eqn} \gamma:\bbr \to Q, \quad t\mapsto \gamma(t), \end{equation}

with $\gamma(0)=q_0$ is interpreted as a time evolution or trajectory of the system, beginning in the configuration $q_0$. However, the configuration space $Q$ often comes with some sort of rule or law, which says that only certain trajectories are allowable or realizable by the system. For example, if we consider a system consisting of a single point mass moving in $3$-space under the influence of an external force, then Newton’s laws of motion dictate which trajectories are allowable in $Q = \bbr^3$ and which are not.

The laws governing the time evolution of a system are often encoded as extra structures on the configuration space $Q$, or on manifolds closely related to $Q$. In fact, the tangent bundle $T(Q)$ often places a central role in dynamical systems theory, due to the following fundamental result which states that smooth paths in the base manifold “lift” to smooth paths in the tangent bundle:

Theorem on Path Lifting. Let $M$ be a manifold. If $\gamma:\bbr \to M$ is a smooth path in $M$, then there is a unique smooth path $\tl{\gamma}: \bbr\to T(M)$ such that the diagram \begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ & \color{white}T(M) \ar@[white][d]^{\color{white}\pi} \\ \color{white}\bbr \ar@[white][r]_{\color{white}\gamma} \ar@[white][ur]^{\color{white}\tl{\gamma}} & \color{white}M } \end{xy} \end{equation} commutes, where $\pi$ is the natural projection.

The theorem is proved by giving an explicit formula for the lift $\tl{\gamma}$, as

\begin{equation}\notag \tl{\gamma}(t) = (\gamma(t), \dot{\gamma}(t)), \end{equation}

where the “dot” denotes a time derivative:

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{\gamma}(t) \defeq \frac{\text{d}\gamma}{\text{d}t}(t). \end{equation}

One may show easily that $\tl{\gamma}$ is smooth, which completes the proof. Q.E.D.

If $Q$ is the configuration space of a dynamical system, then its tangent bundle $T(Q)$ is called the state space of the system, or sometimes the velocity phase space. As we learned in the post on tangent bundles, the generalized position coordinates \eqref{pos-coord-eqn} serve as “horizontal” local coordinates on $T(Q)$, while the remaining coordinates

\begin{equation}\label{vel-coord-eqn} v^1,\ldots,v^n \end{equation}

on $T(Q)$ may be taken as the “vertical” local coordinates. In the physical language, the coordinates \eqref{vel-coord-eqn} are often called generalized velocity coordinates, though they may not literally represent velocities.

The Theorem on Path Lifting shows that time evolutions in a configuration space $Q$, realized as smooth paths in $Q$, may equivalently be realized as time evolutions in the state space $T(Q)$. The advantage of considering the state space in place of the configuration space is that the former allows us to tie together relationships between (generalized) positions and (generalized) velocities in a very natural manner, as both appear as coordinates on the state space. Since the laws that govern the time evolution of a system often depend on both the (generalized) positions and (generalized) velocities of the system, these laws often appear as extra structures on the state space $T(Q)$, rather than $Q$.

For our first example, let’s show that Newton’s Second Law of Motion appears very naturally as a vector field on a state space.

Newton’s Second Law of Motion. Suppose that we have a point mass (i.e., a “particle”) moving in $3$-space under the influence of a force $F$. By fixing an origin and a global coordinate system, we may take the configuration space to be $Q=\bbr^3$. The trajectory of the particle through $Q$ is encoded as a smooth path

\begin{equation}\notag \bbr \to Q, \quad t \mapsto q(t), \end{equation}

which Newton tells us must be a solution to the second-order differential equation

\begin{equation}\notag m\ddot{q}(t) = F(q(t)), \end{equation}

where $m$ is the mass of the particle.

Turning to the state space $T(Q) = \bbr^6$, I claim that Newton’s law manifests itself as the vector field $X$ on $T(Q)$ defined as

\begin{equation}\notag X(q,v) = (v, F(q)/m), \end{equation}

where $q\in \bbr^3$ and $v\in \bbr^3$. Furthermore, I claim that the allowable trajectories in $T(Q)$ are the so-called “integral curves” of $X$. By definition, an integral curve of $X$ is a smooth path $\gamma:\bbr \to T(Q)$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag X(\gamma(t)) = \dot{\gamma}(t) \end{equation}

for all $t$.

For proof, suppose that we decompose a smooth path $\gamma: \bbr \to T(Q)$ in components as

\begin{equation}\notag t\mapsto \gamma(t) = (q(t),v(t)). \end{equation}

If $\gamma$ is an integral curve of $X$ according to the above definition, then the components $q(t)$ and $v(t)$ are solutions to the system of differential equations given by

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{q}(t) = v(t) \quad \text{and} \quad m\dot{v}(t) = F(q(t)). \end{equation}

But this means that $t\mapsto q(t)$ is an allowable trajectory in $Q=\bbr^3$, consistent with Newton’s law.

The example suggests that the differential equations governing newtonian mechanical systems may be described as vector fields on state spaces, and the allowable trajectories are then integral curves of the vector fields. This is an example of an “extra structure” on a state space which encodes the dynamical laws governing a system. But vector fields are not the only types of structures that encode such laws — indeed, as we will see later, other types of dynamical laws arise from smooth functions on state spaces and certain variational principles.

Very roughly, if we take all the allowable trajectories of a given dynamical system, realized as smooth paths, and combine them together into a single object, we obtain the following general type of structure. The definition of this structure applies to general manifolds, not just configuration spaces and state spaces of dynamical systems.

Definition. Let $M$ be a manifold. A one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms of $M$ is a collection of smooth maps

\begin{equation}\notag \theta^t:M \to M, \end{equation}

one for every $t\in \bbr$, such that:

  1. For every $x\in M$, we have $\theta^0(x) = x$.
  2. For all $s,t\in \bbr$, we have $\theta^s \circ \theta^t = \theta^{s+t}$.
  3. The induced map \begin{equation}\label{actionMap-eqn} \theta: \bbr \times M \to M, \quad (t,x) \mapsto \theta(t,x)\defeq \theta^t(x) \end{equation} is smooth.

The identity in part (2.) is called the group law and the induced map \eqref{actionMap-eqn} is called the global flow (or simply flow) associated to the one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms.

Though the manifold $M$ need not be a configuration or state space of a dynamical system, adopting a dynamics-inspired viewpoint is still helpful in understanding the definition of a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms. From this viewpoint, a one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms $\{\theta^t\}$ on a manifold $M$ is the most general type of structure that encodes a dynamical system modelled by $M$. Said differently, the dynamics of a system are often presented as a law that must be “solved” in order to find the allowable trajectories — then, in a sense, one-parameter groups of diffeomorphisms are the “ultimate solutions” to these dynamical laws.

So, given a manifold $M$ with one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms $\{\theta^t\}$, let’s agree to think of the points of $M$ as the “states” of some imaginary dynamical system. Then, given an initial state $x_0\in M$ at time $t=t_0$, for each $t\in \bbr$ the value of $\theta^t(x_0)$ is supposed to represent the state of the system at time $t+t_0$. The identity (1.) is then simply the statement that advancing the system ahead in time by a value $t=0$ doesn’t change the state of the system. In this context, the diffeomorphism $\theta^t$ is often called a $t$-advance operator or an evolution operator.

The group law (2.) is used to show that the system is deterministic, which means the entire evolution of the system is fully determined by the initial state $x_0$ at time $t=t_0$. To see how this works, we advance time to an intermediate value $t=t_1\geq t_0$ and obtain an intermediate state

\begin{equation}\notag x_1 \defeq \theta^{t_1-t_0}(x_0). \end{equation}

After this, suppose we advance time to a final value $t=t_2\geq t_1$, and obtain a final state

\begin{equation}\notag x_2 \defeq \theta^{t_2-t_1}(x_1). \end{equation}

We then have

\begin{equation}\notag x_2 = \theta^{t_2-t_1}(x_1) = \theta^{t_2-t_1}(\theta^{t_1-t_0}(x_0)) = \theta^{t_2-t_0}(x_0), \end{equation}

which shows that the final state $x_2$ does not depend on the intermediate state $x_1$, and is obtained directly from the initial state by advancing time from $t_0$ to $t_2$. The group law is sometimes called the Chapman-Kolmogorov law (see [?]), which is a name that also pops up in the theory of Markov chains (see [?]) where it is used to similar effect.

For each $x\in M$, there is an associated smooth path

\begin{equation}\label{trajectory-eqn} t\mapsto \theta^t(x) \end{equation}

in $M$. If for each such $x$ we define

\begin{equation}\notag X(x) = \frac{d}{dt} \theta^t(x)\big|_{t=0}, \end{equation}

then we obtain a smooth vector field $X$ on $M$ called the infinitesimal generator or the local law of evolution of the global flow. The formal relation between the trajectory \eqref{trajectory-eqn} and the infinitesimal generator is given in the next general definition and theorem. The definition will be familiar from our discussion on Newton’s Second Law of Motion.

Definition. Let $X$ be a smooth vector field on a manifold $M$. A smooth path $\gamma:\bbr \to M$ is called an integral curve of $X$ if \begin{equation}\notag X(\gamma(t)) = \dot{\gamma}(t) \end{equation} for all $t\in \bbr$.

Then, we have:

Theorem on Integral Curves and Infinitesimal Generators. Let $\theta$ be a global flow on $M$ with associated one-parameter group of diffeomorphisms $\{\theta^t\}$. Then, for fixed $x\in M$, the curve $t\mapsto \theta^t(x)$ is an integral curve of the infinitesimal generator $X$.

The proof is just a simple application of the group law and the chain rule. Indeed, for each $t\in \bbr$, we have:

\begin{equation}\notag X(\theta^t(x)) = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}s} \theta^s(\theta^t(x)) \big|_{s=0} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}s} \theta^{s+t}(x) \big|_{s=0} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\theta^t(x). \end{equation}

The desired claim then follows, Q.E.D.

Thus, every global flow on $M$ yields a vector field by passing to the associated infinitesimal generator. Conversely, as we will see below, under certain hypotheses it is possible to reverse this correspondence by associating a global flow with every vector field….

A first glimpse at variational principles: lagrangian mechanics

Our work in the previous section shows that newtonian mechanical systems may be realized as certain vector fields on state space manifolds, and that the evolutions of the systems are described by integral curves of the vector fields. In this section, I want to describe a different (but equivalent) formulation of mechanics called lagrangian mechanics.

The lagrangian formulation has a very different feel to it, compared to the newtonian one. In contrast to the “geometric” flavor of newtonian mechanics, dominated as it is by vector fields and the like, lagrangian mechanics is based fundamentally on a certain variational principle, giving it a distinctly “analytic” feel. Indeed, classical newtonian mechanics is sometimes called vector mechanics, while lagrangian mechanics is sometimes called analytic mechanics. That these two formulations of mechanics are actually equivalent is (I think) quite amazing.

Later, after our brief excursion into lagrangian mechanics, we will come full circle back to vector fields and flows, when we describe the transition from lagrangian mechanics to the (equivalent) formulation of hamiltonian mechanics. The latter is a very (very!) geometric theory — in particular, it is often cited as the primary origin of the mathematical fields of symplectic geometry and topology.

The lagrangian story begins with the following definition, which is so general that it’s hard to imagine it actually has any real content:

Definition. A langrangian system is a pair $(Q,L)$, where $Q$ is a smooth manifold and $L$ is a smooth function $L:T(Q) \to \bbr$. As in the previous section, the manifold $Q$ is called the configuration space of the system and the tangent bundle $T(Q)$ is called the state space of the system (or the velocity phase space). The function $L$ is called the lagrangian of the system.

The points of the state space of a lagrangian system are, as the name suggests, supposed to represent the states of the system. The time evolution of a state is then realized as a smooth path in the state space, beginning at the given state. In contrast to newtonian systems, however, these paths are not (at least at first glance) realized as integral curves of a vector field. Rather, they are described and identified according to the following fundamental variational principle:

Hamilton’s Principle. Let $(Q,L)$ be a lagrangian system. The allowable smooth paths $\gamma:[t_0,t_1]\to Q$ describing the time evolution of the system are exactly those paths that are stationary points of the action integral $A_L$, defined as \begin{equation}\notag A_L(\gamma) = \int_{t_0}^{t_1} L(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t)) \ \text{d}t. \end{equation}

To say that a smooth path $\gamma$ is a stationary point of $A_L$ means that, if we infinitesimally deform $\gamma$ to obtain a second path $\gamma’$, then the two integrals $A_L(\gamma)$ and $A_L(\gamma’)$ do not change. There’s an analogy here with basic one-variable functions: The point $x_0$ is a stationary point of the function $y=f(x)$ precisely when $f’(x_0)=0$, which expresses the fact that (first-order) infinitesimal perturbations away from $x_0$ do not change the value of $f(x_0)$. In our situation, there is a type of derivative of $A_L$ called the first variation, and the path $\gamma$ is a stationary point of $A_L$ if and only if the first variation of $A_L$ vanishes when evaluated at $\gamma$.

I will leave it to you to learn about the formalism of first variations on your own (find a book on the calculus of variations). The only thing that we will need is the fact that stationary points of $A_L$ are solutions to a certain famous system of differential equations:

Theorem on Euler-Lagrange Equations. Let $(Q,L)$ be an $n$-dimensional lagrangian system. A smooth path $\gamma$ in $Q$ is an allowable trajectory of the system according to Hamilton’s Principle if and only if it is a solution to the Euler-Lagrange equations: \begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd L}{\bd q^i}(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t)) = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\bd L}{\bd v^i}(\gamma(t),\dot{\gamma}(t)) \right), \quad i=1,\ldots,n. \end{equation}

These equations are local expressions for the Euler-Lagrange equations, as you can see from the presence of the generalized position coordinates and the generalized velocity coordinates discussed in the previous section. The global Euler-Lagrange equations are obtained from these local expressions by “gluing together on overlaps,” in a suitable sense. We won’t worry too much about this, however, so don’t think too hard about it (if you don’t want to).

The lagrangian $L$ of a lagrangian system is the conceptual analog of the force $F$ of a newtonian system; both of them are the “origin” or the “driver” of the dynamics. While the force $F$ governs the dynamics through Newton’s Second Law, the lagrangian $L$ governs the dynamics through the Euler-Lagrange equations. The link between the two formulations of mechanics is made when we assume that $F$ is conservative, with potential $V$ (i.e., $F = -\nabla V$), and we define a lagrangian $L$ by

\begin{equation}\label{lagrangian-eqn} L = K - V, \end{equation}

where $K$ is the kinetic energy of the system. For simplicity, let’s suppose that our system consists of a single particle of mass $m$ moving in the configuration space $Q=\bbr^3$, subject to the force $F$. Then, we have

\begin{equation}\notag L(q,v) = \frac{1}{2}m||v||^2 - V(q). \end{equation}

If

\begin{equation}\notag \gamma: \bbr \to Q, \quad t\mapsto \gamma(t) = (\gamma^1(t),\gamma^2(t),\gamma^3(t)) \end{equation}

is the trajectory of the particle, then the Euler-Lagrange equations take the form

\begin{equation}\notag -\frac{\bd V}{\bd q^i}(\gamma(t)) = m\ddot{\gamma}^i(t) \quad (i=1,2,3). \end{equation}

But these are exactly (the components of) Newton’s Second Law. While this argument shows that newtonian mechanics may be reformulated in the lagrangian framework only for this very special dynamical system consisting of a single particle, the equivalence of the two formulations is true much more generally.

Let’s consider an example where the configuration space $Q$ is non-euclidean. Suppose that we have a particle $p$ constrained to move along the sphere $S^2\subset \bbr^3$, acted on by gravity and the constraining force:

We have coordinatized the system so that the equatorial plane of $S^2$ is the $xy$-plane, while the positive $z$-axis points downward, in the direction of gravity. We let $\theta$ and $\varphi$ denote the spherical coordinates of the particle $p$, shown as the white angles in the figure. The angle $\theta$ is the azimuthal angle measured from the positive $x$-axis, while $\varphi$ is the polar angle measured from the positive $z$-axis. While $p$ moves in the ambient euclidean space $\bbr^3$, the only physically realizable positions lie on the sphere $S^2$, and hence the configuration space of the system is $Q = S^2$. For mathematical simplicity, we will suppose that all our constants are “geometric,” so that the particle has unit mass and the acceleration due to gravity is $g=1$.

The state space is the tangent bundle $T(S^2)$, which is a submanifold of the “ambient” state space $T(\bbr^3) = \bbr^6$. The kinetic energy of $p$ computed in the global euclidean coordinates of $T(\bbr^3)$ is given by the familiar formula

\begin{equation}\notag K(x^1,x^2,x^3,v^1,v^2,v^3) = \frac{1}{2}\big((v^1)^2 + (v^2)^2 + (v^3)^2\big), \end{equation}

while the gravitational potential energy is given by

\begin{equation}\notag V(x^1,x^2,x^3,v^1,v^2,v^3) = -z + 1, \end{equation}

where I’ve taken $V=0$ at the equilibrium position $(0,0,1)$. We may restrict $K$ and $V$ to obtain smooth functions

\begin{equation}\notag K,V: T(S^2) \to \bbr, \end{equation}

and then the lagrangian $L:T(S^2) \to \bbr$ is defined above as the difference $L= K-V$. Notice that I have effectively ignored the constraining force that keeps the particle on the sphere $S^2$; this constraining force is taken into account when we declare the configuration space to be $Q=S^2$.

Now, in order to obtain the allowable trajectories of the system, we must express the Euler-Lagrange equations in local coordinates on the state space $T(S^2)$. To do so, we first recall that the map

\begin{equation}\notag (\theta,\varphi) \mapsto (\cos{\theta}\sin{\varphi},\sin{\theta}\sin{\varphi},\cos{\varphi}) \end{equation}

provides a local parametrization of $S^2$, suitably restricted to an open subset of ordered pairs $(\theta,\varphi)\in \bbr^2$. It follows from our previous post on tangent bundles, that the global pushforward of this map provides a local parametrization of the tangent bundle $T(S^2)$; if we write $\theta,\varphi$ and $\dot{\theta},\dot{\varphi}$ as the corresponding local coordinates on the tangent bundle, then I will leave it to you to check that we have

\begin{equation}\notag K(\theta,\varphi,\dot{\theta},\dot{\varphi}) = \frac{1}{2}(\dot{\varphi}^2 +\dot{\theta}^2\sin^2{(\varphi)}) \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag V(\theta,\varphi,\dot{\theta},\dot{\varphi}) = -\cos{\varphi}+1 \end{equation}

in these coordinates. Here, $\dot{\theta}$ and $\dot{\varphi}$ do not represent time derivatives; rather, they are purely formal symbols that make it clear that $\dot{\theta}$ is the (generalized) velocity coordinate corresponding to the (generalized) position coordinate $\theta$, and similarly for $\dot{\varphi}$ and $\varphi$.

If the local coordinates are functions of time, representing an allowable trajectory of the particle in state space $T(S^2)$, then they must be solutions to the Euler-Lagrange equations

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\bd L}{\bd \theta} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{\theta}} \right) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\bd L}{\bd \varphi} = \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\bd L}{\bd \dot{\varphi}} \right). \end{equation}

Carrying through with the differentiations, the first equation yields

\begin{equation}\notag 0 = \ddot{\theta}\sin^2{\varphi} + 2\dot{\theta}\dot{\varphi}\sin{\varphi}\cos{\varphi} \end{equation}

while the second equation yields

\begin{equation}\notag \dot{\theta}^2 \sin{\varphi}\cos{\varphi} - \sin{\varphi} = \ddot{\varphi}. \end{equation}

The cotangent bundle

It will be convenient to begin in the “local case,” where we assume that our configuration space is $Q = \bbr^n$, and our state space is the tangent bundle $T(Q) = \bbr^{2n}$. We suppose that the global coordinates of $T(Q)$ are given by

\begin{equation}\notag q^1,\ldots,q^n,v^1,\ldots,v^n, \end{equation}

where, in the geometric language, $q^1,\ldots,q^n$ are the horizontal coordinates and $v^1,\ldots,v^n$ are the vertical coordinates. In the physical language, $q^1,\ldots,q^n$ are the generalized position coordinates and $v^1,\ldots,v^n$ are the generalized velocity coordinates.

Let $Q$ be an $n$-dimensional manifold embedded in $\bbr^r$, and consider the natural projection $\pi: T(Q) \to Q$. Clearly the pushforward $\pi_{\ast,(q,v)}$ is surjective at every point $(q,v) \in T(Q)$, and hence $\pi$ is a submersion. For each $q\in Q$, it then follows that the fiber

\begin{equation}\notag \pi^{-1}(q) = \{q\}\times T_q(Q) \end{equation}

is an $n$-dimensional (closed) submanifold of the tangent bundle $T(Q)$. In fact, the fiber $\pi^{-1}(q)$, as a smooth submanifold of $T(Q)$, has global coordinates given by the vertical coordinates $v^1,\ldots,v^n$ over an open parametrizable subset of $q\in Q$. In particular, there is an isomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag T_q(Q) \xrightarrow{\cong} T_{(q,v)}(\pi^{-1}(q)) \end{equation}

of vector spaces that does…

\begin{equation} T_q(Q) \xrightarrow{\cong} T_{(q,v)} \left( \pi^{-1}(q)\right), \quad w\mapsto (0,w) \end{equation}

of vector spaces.

Now, let $L:T(Q) \to \bbr$ be a smooth function. Since the fiber $\pi^{-1}(q)$ is a smooth submanifold of $T(Q)$, the restriction

\begin{equation}\notag L|_{\pi^{-1}(q)}: \pi^{-1}(q) \xrightarrow{\text{inclusion}} T(Q) \xrightarrow{L} \bbr \end{equation}

is smooth. If we pass to the differential at a point $(q,v)$ in the fiber, we get a linear map

\begin{equation}\notag \text{d}\left( L|_{\pi^{-1}(q)} \right)_{(q,v)}: T_{(q,v)}(\pi^{-1}(q)) \to \bbr. \end{equation}

However, as was saw above, we may use the global coordinates on $T_q(Q)$ to identify the double tangent space $T_v(T_q(Q))$ with $T_q(Q)$, and thus this last map may be identified with a covector

\begin{equation}\notag \text{d}\left( L|_{T_q(Q)} \right)_v: T_q(Q) \to \bbr \end{equation}

in the cotangent space $T^q(Q)$.

Definition. Let the notation be as above. The smooth map \begin{equation}\notag FL: T(Q) \to T^\ast(Q), \quad (q,v) \mapsto \left(q, \text{d}\left( L|_{T_q(Q)} \right)_v\right) \end{equation} is called the fiber derivative or Legendre transform of $L$.

It will be helpful to find a local expression for the fiber derivative $FL$. To do so, let $q^1,\ldots,q^n,v^1,\ldots,v^n$ be the horizontal/vertical coordinates on $T(Q)$ above an open parametrizable subset of $q$, and let $q^1,\ldots,q^n,p_1,\ldots,p_n$ be the horizontal/vertical coordinates on $T^\ast(Q)$. Recall that

\begin{equation}\notag p^i = \text{d}q_i \end{equation}

We have that

\begin{equation}\notag \text{d}\left( L|_{T_q(Q)} \right) = \frac{\bd L}{\bd v^i} \text{d}v^i \end{equation}

A first glimpse at symplectic topology: hamiltonian mechanics
